title: Quiz: Quantum Discriminators in QGANs
description: Test your understanding of quantum and hybrid discriminator design and implementation

questions.question: What is the primary role of a discriminator in a GAN?

options.text: Distinguish real data from generated data

questions.explanation: The discriminator's role is to classify inputs as real (from the training data) or fake (from the generator), providing the adversarial signal that trains the generator.

questions.question: Why are hybrid quantum-classical discriminators often preferred over fully quantum discriminators?

options.text: They are faster on classical computers
They combine quantum feature extraction with classical classification power
They don't require any quantum hardware

questions.explanation: Hybrid discriminators leverage quantum circuits for feature extraction (potentially capturing quantum correlations) while using classical neural networks for flexible, high-capacity classification.

questions.question: What does a quantum kernel discriminator compute?

options.text: The quantum entropy of the input

questions.explanation: Quantum kernel methods compute similarity between inputs by encoding them into quantum states and measuring their inner product: K(x,x') = |⟨φ(x)|φ(x')⟩|².

questions.question: How is the discriminator output typically obtained from a quantum circuit?

options.text: Expectation value of an observable like ⟨Z₀⟩

questions.explanation: The discriminator output is computed as the expectation value of an observable (typically Pauli-Z on the first qubit), mapped to [0,1] via (⟨Z⟩ + 1)/2.

questions.question: In a quantum discriminator, amplitude encoding can encode 2^n features using only n qubits.
questions.explanation: True. Amplitude encoding represents data in the amplitudes of a quantum state, allowing 2^n features to be encoded in n qubits, though preparing the state can be expensive.

questions.question: What is a key challenge when training quantum discriminators?

options.text: Barren plateaus in the loss landscape

questions.explanation: Barren plateaus cause gradients to vanish exponentially with circuit size, making training difficult. Strategies like local cost functions and careful initialization help mitigate this.

questions.question: Why might you use label smoothing when training a quantum discriminator?

options.text: To prevent overconfident predictions and improve stability
To reduce the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: Label smoothing replaces hard labels (0,1) with soft labels (0.1, 0.9), preventing the discriminator from becoming overconfident and improving training stability.

questions.question: Which are valid approaches for extracting discriminator outputs from quantum circuits? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Weighted sum of multiple qubit expectations

questions.explanation: All methods are valid: (a) single-qubit measurement, (b) global parity measurement, (c) probability-based output, and (d) weighted observables provide different trade-offs for discriminator design.
